message.content:
Research Unix/Linux file naming conventions and patterns for configuration files.

Focus on:
1. Dotfile patterns:
   - .file (hidden)
   - .filerc (run commands)
   - .file.d/ (directory of configs)
   - file.conf
   - file.cfg

2. Special directory patterns:
   - /etc/X.d/ (modular configs)
   - ~/.config/X/ (XDG standard)
   - .local/ hierarchy

3. Semantic naming patterns:
   - MANIFEST
   - Makefile
   - Dockerfile
   - Vagrantfile
   - Procfile
   - CODEOWNERS
   - CLAUDE.md

4. What pattern would be most semantic for "agent rules"?
   - .agentrc
   - .agent/rules
   - AGENTRULES
   - agent.rules
   - .rules
   - __rules__ (Python dunder style)

Return analysis of which patterns are most recognized and semantic.

content.text: Vou pesquisar as convenções de nomenclatura de arquivos Unix/Linux para configurações e analisar qual padrão seria mais semântico para "agent rules".

input.query: Unix Linux dotfile naming conventions .rc .conf .d directory patterns
XDG Base Directory Specification config file naming standard
semantic config file naming Makefile Dockerfile Procfile pattern convention

content.content:
Web search results for query: "semantic config file naming Makefile Dockerfile Procfile pattern convention"

Links: [{"title":"Use <purpose>.dockerfile naming convention · Issue #9245 · docker/docs","url":"https://github.com/docker/docker.github.io/issues/9245"},{"title":"Docker Style Guide - Broad Institute Long Read Pipelines","url":"https://broadinstitute.github.io/long-read-pipelines/development_guide/docker_style_guide/"},{"title":"Best Practices for Writing Dockerfiles.","url":"https://www.xcubelabs.com/blog/best-practices-for-writing-dockerfiles/"},{"title":"Dockerfile naming convention · Issue #15549 · moby/moby","url":"https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/15549"},{"title":"File Naming Conventions","url":"https://yourfilenameiswrong.com/"},{"title":"Dockerfile overview | Docker Docs","url":"https://docs.docker.com/build/concepts/dockerfile/"},{"title":"A Reusable Makefile To Build And Release Docker Images | Xebia","url":"https://xebia.com/blog/makefile-for-docker-images/"},{"title":"Dockerfile Naming Convention and Organization – mohitgoyal.co","url":"https://mohitgoyal.co/2018/08/29/dockerfile-naming-convention-and-organization/"},{"title":"Simpler Dev Environments with Procfiles","url":"https://meijer.works/articles/simpler-dev-environments-with-procfiles/"},{"title":"Codemia | Master System Design Interviews Through Active Practice","url":"https://codemia.io/knowledge-hub/path/how_to_name_dockerfiles"}]

Based on my search, here's what I found about the naming conventions for semantic configuration files like Makefile, Dockerfile, and Procfile:

## The "Purposefile" Pattern

These files follow a naming convention where the filename itself describes the file's purpose or format, rather than using a traditional extension. This pattern includes:

- **Makefile** - Build automation instructions
- **Dockerfile** - Container build instructions  
- **Procfile** - Process declarations for platforms like Heroku

### Key Conventions

A Procfile uses a simple key: command format, and much like a Dockerfile, it's by convention named after the format.(no content)When you are building an image using Dockerfile, by default it would search for a file named Dockerfile with no file extension in the current directory context.

### Multiple Files of the Same Type

When projects need multiple variations, there are different approaches:

Some projects may need distinct Dockerfiles for specific purposes. A common convention is to name these `<something>.Dockerfile`.(no content)Docker recommends using the default (Dockerfile) for your project's primary Dockerfile.

### The Extension Debate

There's an ongoing discussion about whether these should be treated as extensions:

"We often see patterns like .env.development instead of development.env. .env, .dockerfile and others are actually file extensions, and it's a mystery why people start to suffix the file type."(no content)The .dockerfile extension already works for syntax highlighting in VS Code using the glob pattern. Using a lowercase .dockerfile would match with general naming convention for other extensions, while keeping Dockerfile for extensionless naming when only one Dockerfile is required for your project.(no content)Proper naming allows files to be grouped by extension first, then sorted by their purpose, making them easier to find in directories with many files.

### Best Practices

Adopt consistent naming conventions for Dockerfiles, making it easy for developers to locate the appropriate file for a specific service or component. Consider using a standardized prefix or suffix to distinguish Dockerfiles based on context.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "XDG Base Directory Specification config file naming standard"

Links: [{"title":"XDG Base Directory - ArchWiki","url":"https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/XDG_Base_Directory"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory Specification","url":"https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir/latest/"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory Specification | Alchemists","url":"https://alchemists.io/articles/xdg_base_directory_specification"},{"title":"Use the XDG Base Directory Specification!","url":"https://xdgbasedirectoryspecification.com/"},{"title":"XDGBaseDirectorySpecification - Debian Wiki","url":"https://wiki.debian.org/XDGBaseDirectorySpecification"},{"title":"XDG/Base Directories - Gentoo wiki","url":"https://wiki.gentoo.org/wiki/XDG/Base_Directories"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory Specification","url":"https://developer-old.gnome.org/basedir-spec/"},{"title":"XDG Base Directory :: IT'S FOSS","url":"https://itsfoss.gitlab.io/blog/xdg-base-directory/"},{"title":"XDG cheat sheet · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/roalcantara/107ba66dfa3b9d023ac9329e639bc58c"},{"title":"Base Directories — PyXDG 0.26 documentation","url":"https://pyxdg.readthedocs.io/en/latest/basedirectory.html"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the XDG Base Directory Specification and config file naming standards:

## XDG Base Directory Specification Overview

The XDG Base Directory Specification defines an organized folder and file structure for applications to store associated user configuration, cache, data, state, and runtime information on UNIX-like systems. This allows for consistency across different programs and desktops.

### Key Environment Variables and Default Paths

$XDG_CONFIG_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific configuration files should be stored.(no content)By default, $XDG_CONFIG_HOME is set to ~/.config.(no content)$XDG_DATA_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific data files should be stored. If $XDG_DATA_HOME is either not set or empty, a default equal to $HOME/.local/share should be used.(no content)XDG_STATE_HOME is the base directory relative to which user-specific state files should be stored, containing data that should persist between (application) restarts, but that is not important or portable enough to the user that it should be stored in XDG_DATA_HOME. If not set, or empty, assumed to be $HOME/.local/state/.(no content)$XDG_CACHE_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific non-essential data files should be stored. If $XDG_CACHE_HOME is either not set or empty, a default equal to $HOME/.cache should be used.(no content)$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR defines the base directory relative to which user-specific non-essential runtime files and other file objects (such as sockets, named pipes, ...) should be stored.

### Config File Naming Standard

Applications should use a $HOME/.config/<application>/configuration.yml structure. The <application> sub-folder is important because it keeps the XDG .config folder organized so each application is clearly identified.(no content)Application-specific configuration files are typically located within subdirectories of this directory, following the application's name or a similar identifier. For instance, the configuration file for the text editor nano might be found at ~/.config/nano/config.(no content)The 'resource' should normally be the name of your application.(no content)The configuration doesn't have to be YAML but could be INI, TOML, or whatever format best suits the needs of the application.

### Path Requirements

All paths set in these environment variables must be absolute. If an implementation encounters a relative path in any of these variables it should consider the path invalid and ignore it.(no content)Specifications may reference this specification by specifying the location of a configuration file as $XDG_CONFIG_DIRS/subdir/filename. This implies that default configuration files should be installed to $sysconfdir/xdg/subdir/filename with $sysconfdir defaulting to /etc. A user specific version of the configuration file may be created in $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/subdir/filename.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

toolUseResult.query: XDG Base Directory Specification config file naming standard

content.title: Use the XDG Base Directory Specification!

## XDG Base Directory Specification Overview

The XDG Base Directory Specification defines an organized folder and file structure for applications to store associated user configuration, cache, data, state, and runtime information on UNIX-like systems. This allows for consistency across different programs and desktops.

### Key Environment Variables and Default Paths

$XDG_CONFIG_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific configuration files should be stored.(no content)By default, $XDG_CONFIG_HOME is set to ~/.config.(no content)$XDG_DATA_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific data files should be stored. If not set, or empty, assumed to be $HOME/.local/state/.(no content)$XDG_CACHE_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific non-essential data files should be stored. If $XDG_CACHE_HOME is either not set or empty, a default equal to $HOME/.cache should be used.(no content)$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR defines the base directory relative to which user-specific non-essential runtime files and other file objects (such as sockets, named pipes, ...) should be stored.

### Config File Naming Standard

Applications should use a $HOME/.config/<application>/configuration.yml structure. The <application> sub-folder is important because it keeps the XDG .config folder organized so each application is clearly identified.(no content)Application-specific configuration files are typically located within subdirectories of this directory, following the application's name or a similar identifier. For instance, the configuration file for the text editor nano might be found at ~/.config/nano/config.(no content)The 'resource' should normally be the name of your application.(no content)The configuration doesn't have to be YAML but could be INI, TOML, or whatever format best suits the needs of the application.

Links: [{"title":"What Does 'rc' Mean in Dot Files? Linux Configuration Files Explained — linuxvox.com","url":"https://linuxvox.com/blog/what-does-rc-mean-in-dot-files/"},{"title":"File and Directory Names - Learning the UNIX Operating System, Fourth Edition [Book]","url":"https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/learning-the-unix/1565923901/ch04s02.html"},{"title":"Linux / UNIX: Rules For Naming File And Directory Names - nixCraft","url":"https://www.cyberciti.biz/faq/linuxunix-rules-for-naming-file-and-directory-names/"},{"title":"The 7 Main Rules For Naming Files and Folders in Linux and Unix - LinuxG.net","url":"https://linuxg.net/the-7-main-rules-for-naming-files-and-folders-in-linux-and-unix/"},{"title":"[SOLVED] Linux configuration files naming convention","url":"https://www.linuxquestions.org/questions/linux-newbie-8/linux-configuration-files-naming-convention-4175645690/"},{"title":"Linux file System and Naming Convention Explained","url":"https://www.computernetworkingnotes.com/linux-tutorials/linux-file-system-and-naming-convention-explained.html"},{"title":"04-E.12: Linux Directory Structure - Engineering LibreTexts","url":"https://eng.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Computer_Science/Operating_Systems/Linux_-_The_Penguin_Marches_On_(McClanahan)/04:_Managing_Linux_Storage/5.12:_Linux_Directory_Structure"},{"title":"Naming Conventions—UNIX or Linux","url":"https://ftpdocs.broadcom.com/cadocs/0/CA XCOM Data Transport for UNIX and Linux 11 5-ENU/Bookshelf_Files/HTML/CA_XCOM_Overview_UNIX_Linux_ENU/978171.html"},{"title":"Dead-simple dotfiles Management","url":"https://konfekt.github.io/blog/2018/11/10/simple-dotfiles-setup"},{"title":"Configuration file - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Configuration_file"}]

Here's what I found about Unix/Linux dotfile naming conventions:

## Dotfiles (Hidden Files)

Dot files (or "hidden files") are configuration files in Unix-like systems whose names start with a dot (.). This naming convention hides them by default from standard ls commands (you'll need `ls -a` to list them).(no content)They live in your home directory (~) and control the behavior of applications, shells, and system tools.

## The `.rc` Suffix

The 'rc' suffix is ubiquitous in dot files, but its origin is surprisingly debated. The most widely accepted explanation traces 'rc' to "Run Commands", a term from early computing history.(no content)When Unix was developed in the 1970s, its creators (Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie) borrowed the "run commands" idea. The system's startup script was named /etc/rc (still used today in some Linux distros), short for "run commands." Over time, this convention trickled down to user-specific configuration files like .bashrc and .vimrc.(no content)A common misconception is that 'rc' stands for "Resource Configuration" or "Runtime Configuration." While these descriptions fit what rc files do (they configure resources or runtime behavior), they are not the original origin. This is a retrospective reinterpretation of the acronym.(no content)Similar to config files, a run command (rc) shell script can configure a shell session. Often, such scripts are named with an rc suffix after the consuming program's name such as .xinitrc, .vimrc, .bashrc, xsane.rc.

## The `.d` Directory Pattern

You can avoid monolithic rc files by splitting settings into smaller files in a .d directory. This pattern allows modular configuration where multiple files in a directory are processed together (e.g., `/etc/conf.d/`, `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/`).

## Configuration File Locations

System-wide software often uses configuration files stored in /etc, while user applications often use a "dotfile" – a file or directory in the home directory prefixed with a period, which in Unix hides the file or directory from casual listing. Since this causes pollution, newer user applications generally make their own folder in the .config directory, a standardized subdirectory of the home directory.

## Best Practices

Store dotfiles in a Git repository (e.g., ~/.dotfiles) to track changes, revert mistakes, and sync across machines.(no content)On UNIX based operating systems, such as Linux, Free BSD and MacOS, it is common practice to put one's dotfiles under version control. Newer practices involve moving dotfiles to the $XDG_CONFIG_HOME folder (usually ~/.config) and creating symlinks to them.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "CODEOWNERS MANIFEST OWNERS file naming convention uppercase semantic files"

Links: [{"title":"Syntax of `CODEOWNERS` file | GitLab Docs","url":"https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/codeowners/reference/"},{"title":"Understanding GitHub CODEOWNERS","url":"https://graphite.com/guides/in-depth-guide-github-codeowners"},{"title":"Code Owners | GitLab Docs","url":"https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/codeowners/"},{"title":"Set up and use code owners | Bitbucket Cloud | Atlassian Support","url":"https://support.atlassian.com/bitbucket-cloud/docs/set-up-and-use-code-owners/"},{"title":"Define code owners","url":"https://admhelp.microfocus.com/pulseuno/en/24.2-25.2/Online/Content/UserHelp/codeowners-file.htm"},{"title":"The Ultimate Guide to GitHub Codeowners File | Graph AI","url":"https://www.graphapp.ai/blog/the-ultimate-guide-to-github-codeowners-file"},{"title":"About code owners - GitHub Docs","url":"https://docs.github.com/articles/about-code-owners"},{"title":"The Ultimate CODEOWNERS File Guide for Better Code Reviews - Aviator Blog","url":"https://www.aviator.co/blog/a-modern-guide-to-codeowners/"},{"title":"Advanced `CODEOWNERS` configuration | GitLab Docs","url":"https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/codeowners/advanced/"},{"title":"A Comprehensive Guide to CODEOWNERS in GitHub - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/eunice-js/a-comprehensive-guide-to-codeowners-in-github-22ga"}]

Based on my search results, here's what I found about CODEOWNERS and related file naming conventions:

## CODEOWNERS File

You can use a CODEOWNERS file to define individuals or teams that are responsible for code in a repository.(no content)A GitHub Codeowners file is a plain text file named "CODEOWNERS" that resides in the root of a repository.

### File Location
To use a CODEOWNERS file, create a new file called CODEOWNERS in the .github/, root, or docs/ directory of the repository, in the branch where you'd like to add the code owners.(no content)If CODEOWNERS files exist in more than one of those locations, GitHub will search for them in that order and use the first one it finds.

For GitLab, GitLab checks these locations in your repository in this order. The first CODEOWNERS file found is used, and all others are ignored: In the root directory: ./CODEOWNERS. In the docs directory: ./docs/CODEOWNERS. In the .gitlab directory: ./.gitlab/CODEOWNERS.

For Bitbucket, Create the CODEOWNERS file in a directory called .bitbucket at the root of your repository.

### Key Features
- Last matching pattern takes precedence: When multiple patterns apply to the same file, the last pattern listed in the CODEOWNERS file takes precedence. This means that if two patterns refer to the same file, the owner specified in the pattern that appears later in the file will be responsible for reviewing changes to that file.
- CODEOWNERS files must be under 3 MB in size.

### Uppercase Naming Convention
The CODEOWNERS file follows the convention of using ALL UPPERCASE letters for semantic/special files in repositories (similar to README, LICENSE, CONTRIBUTING, MANIFEST, etc.). This convention helps these important meta-files stand out in directory listings and signals their special purpose in the repository structure.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Single and Double Underscores in Python Names – Real Python","url":"https://realpython.com/python-double-underscore/"},{"title":"Dunder (Double UNDERscore) Alias","url":"https://wiki.python.org/moin/DunderAlias"},{"title":"PEP 8 – Style Guide for Python Code | peps.python.org","url":"https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/"},{"title":"The Meaning of Underscores in Python – dbader.org","url":"https://dbader.org/blog/meaning-of-underscores-in-python"},{"title":"Underscores, dunders and everything nice","url":"https://www.hacksoft.io/blog/underscores-dunders-and-everything-nice"},{"title":"Getting started with classes in Python Part 8: \"dunder\" | Ansys Developer Portal","url":"https://developer.ansys.com/blog/getting-started-classes-python-part-8-dunder"},{"title":"Python and the underscore (_) | Towards Data Science","url":"https://towardsdatascience.com/python-and-the-underscore-82d7ce8706d/"},{"title":"What does double underscore prefix do in Python variables?","url":"https://www.tutorialspoint.com/What-does-double-underscore-prefix-do-in-Python-variables"},{"title":"Exploring Double Underscore (__) Files and Identifiers in Python | by Chris Yan | Medium","url":"https://chrisyandata.medium.com/exploring-double-underscore-files-and-identifiers-in-python-2a6346e2d3d2"},{"title":"Underscore in Python Tutorial : What is the purpose and meaning of _ & __ | DataCamp","url":"https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/role-underscore-python"}]

Here's what I found about Python's double underscore (dunder) naming conventions:

## Python Dunder Naming Convention

### What is a "Dunder"?
"Dunder" is short for "double underscore." So `__init__` is pronounced "dunder init dunder", or just "dunder init".(no content)Double underscores are often referred to as "dunders" in the Python community. The reason is that double underscores appear quite often in Python code and to avoid fatiguing their jaw muscles, Pythonistas often shorten "double underscore" to "dunder."

### Types of Dunder Names

**1. Magic/Special Methods (`__name__`)**
Double leading and trailing underscores—known as dunders—denote special methods or attributes, such as `.__init__()`, `.__len__()`, and `__name__`, which Python uses to support internal behaviors.(no content)`__double_leading_and_trailing_underscore__`: "magic" objects or attributes that live in user-controlled namespaces. E.g. `__init__`, `__import__` or `__file__`. Never invent such names; only use them as documented.

**2. Special Files**
The `__init__.py` file marks a directory as a Python package and can contain initialization code for the package. It enables the directory's modules to be imported as part of the package.(no content)The `__main__.py` file allows a module or package to be executed as a script.

**3. The `__name__` Variable**
When code is run as a script `__name__` is set to `'__main__'`, which is the name of the top-level environment of the program. When code is imported as a module, `__name__` is set to the name of the module file.(no content)This name-main idiom allows you to execute code when you run the Python file as a script, but not when you import it as a module.

### Name Mangling (Double Leading Underscore Only)
Python's name mangling automatically renames attributes or methods with double leading underscores by prefixing them with the class name, helping you avoid accidental overrides in subclasses.(no content)It's important to note that Python doesn't name-mangle dunder names, even though they start with two leading underscores. That means you can do something like `cart.__len__()`, and it'll work.

### PEP 8 Guidelines
Module level "dunders" (i.e. names with two leading and two trailing underscores) such as `__all__`, `__author__`, `__version__`, etc. should be placed after the module docstring but before any import statements except from `__future__` imports.

### Best Practices
It's best to stay away from using names that start and end with double underscores ("dunders") in your own programs to avoid collisions with future changes to the Python language.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

The Meaning of Underscores in Python – dbader.org
Getting started with classes in Python Part 8: "dunder" | Ansys Developer Portal
Python and the underscore (_) | Towards Data Science

results:
Here's what I found about Python's double underscore (dunder) naming conventions:

## Python Dunder Naming Convention

### What is a "Dunder"?
"Dunder" is short for "double underscore." So `__init__` is pronounced "dunder init dunder", or just "dunder init".(no content)Double underscores are often referred to as "dunders" in the Python community. The reason is that double underscores appear quite often in Python code and to avoid fatiguing their jaw muscles, Pythonistas often shorten "double underscore" to "dunder."

### Types of Dunder Names

**1. Magic/Special Methods (`__name__`)**
Double leading and trailing underscores—known as dunders—denote special methods or attributes, such as `.__init__()`, `.__len__()`, and `__name__`, which Python uses to support internal behaviors.(no content)`__double_leading_and_trailing_underscore__`: "magic" objects or attributes that live in user-controlled namespaces. E.g. `__init__`, `__import__` or `__file__`. Never invent such names; only use them as documented.

**2. Special Files**
The `__init__.py` file marks a directory as a Python package and can contain initialization code for the package. It enables the directory's modules to be imported as part of the package.(no content)The `__main__.py` file allows a module or package to be executed as a script.

**3. The `__name__` Variable**
When code is run as a script `__name__` is set to `'__main__'`, which is the name of the top-level environment of the program. When code is imported as a module, `__name__` is set to the name of the module file.(no content)This name-main idiom allows you to execute code when you run the Python file as a script, but not when you import it as a module.

### Name Mangling (Double Leading Underscore Only)
Python's name mangling automatically renames attributes or methods with double leading underscores by prefixing them with the class name, helping you avoid accidental overrides in subclasses.(no content)It's important to note that Python doesn't name-mangle dunder names, even though they start with two leading underscores. That means you can do something like `cart.__len__()`, and it'll work.

### PEP 8 Guidelines
Module level "dunders" (i.e. names with two leading and two trailing underscores) such as `__all__`, `__author__`, `__version__`, etc. should be placed after the module docstring but before any import statements except from `__future__` imports.

### Best Practices
It's best to stay away from using names that start and end with double underscores ("dunders") in your own programs to avoid collisions with future changes to the Python language.

content.content:
Web search results for query: ".editorconfig .eslintrc .prettierrc developer tooling config file naming"

Links: [{"title":"Configuration File · Prettier","url":"https://prettier.io/docs/configuration"},{"title":"Set up ESlint, Prettier & EditorConfig without conflicts | Theodo","url":"https://blog.theodo.com/2019/08/empower-your-dev-environment-with-eslint-prettier-and-editorconfig-with-no-conflicts/"},{"title":"Survivejs","url":"https://survivejs.com/books/maintenance/code-quality/code-formatting/"},{"title":"How I Setup ESLint, Prettier, and EditorConfig for Static Sites - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/npranto/how-i-setup-eslint-prettier-and-editorconfig-for-static-sites-33ep"},{"title":"Why You Should Use ESLint, Prettier & EditorConfig | Theodo","url":"https://blog.theodo.com/2019/08/why-you-should-use-eslint-prettier-and-editorconfig-together/"},{"title":"API · Prettier","url":"https://prettier.io/docs/api.html"},{"title":"EditorConfig vs. ESLint vs. Prettier: Do You Need All Three? A Guide to Code Consistency Tools for Developers — w3tutorials.net","url":"https://www.w3tutorials.net/blog/editorconfig-vs-eslint-vs-prettier-is-it-worthwhile-to-use-them-all/"},{"title":"\"Once and for all\" editorconfig + prettier + eslint + husky + lint staged configuration","url":"https://qdmana.com/2021/08/20210831233810586j.html"},{"title":"Only 1% of Developers Know How to configure Prettier properly. - Skill Stuff","url":"https://skillstuff.com/only-1-of-developers-know-how-to-configure-prettier-properly/"},{"title":"Setup .eslint, .prettier, .editorconfig, .jsconfig in react apps","url":"https://mepritam.dev/references/setup-eslint-prettier-editorconfig-jsconfig-in-react-apps/"}]

Based on my search results, here's information about `.editorconfig`, `.eslintrc`, and `.prettierrc` developer tooling config file naming conventions:

## Config File Naming Conventions

### EditorConfig
EditorConfig uses a plain text file named `.editorconfig` in your project root. This file defines rules for specific file types (e.g., .js, .py) or directories.(no content)EditorConfig allows you to define indentation style and other whitespace settings for any file type. This way your editor can automatically choose the correct settings. This is handy when developers use platforms with different line endings, e.g., Mac and Windows.

### ESLint
ESLint's configuration is provided within a `.eslintrc.*` file (can be `.eslintrc.json` or `.eslint.yaml` …) that can be used to define your own set of rules or to use plugins available for different frameworks (React, Vue, TypeScript).

### Prettier
Prettier supports: A `.prettierrc` file written in JSON or YAML; a `.prettierrc.json`, `.prettierrc.yml`, `.prettierrc.yaml`, or `.prettierrc.json5` file; a `.prettierrc.js`, `prettier.config.js`, `.prettierrc.ts`, or `prettier.config.ts` file that exports an object using export default or module.exports.(no content)Additional supported formats include: a `.prettierrc.mjs`, `prettier.config.mjs`, `.prettierrc.mts`, or `prettier.config.mts` file that exports an object using export default; a `.prettierrc.cjs`, `prettier.config.cjs`, `.prettierrc.cts`, or `prettier.config.cts` file that exports an object using module.exports; a `.prettierrc.toml` file.

## How They Work Together

If a `.editorconfig` file is in your project, Prettier will parse it and convert its properties to the corresponding Prettier configuration. This configuration will be overridden by `.prettierrc`, etc.(no content)EditorConfig and Prettier serve different purposes: EditorConfig's role is to configure your editor so that the code you write is already formatted while Prettier will format your already written code.(no content)EditorConfig, ESLint, and Prettier each solve distinct problems: EditorConfig ensures basic text consistency across editors. ESLint catches bugs and enforces code quality in JavaScript/TypeScript. Prettier handles uniform formatting across languages.

## Best Practices

ESLint's style rules (e.g., indent, quotes) often conflict with Prettier. Use `eslint-config-prettier` to disable ESLint's formatting rules, letting Prettier own formatting.(no content)Whenever you want to add a rule, try and categorize the rule within the code quality or code formatting sets. To make this easier, you just have to check whether the rule is supported and enforceable by Prettier.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "AI agent configuration file naming convention LLM rules prompt"

Links: [{"title":"Some notes on AI Agent Rule / Instruction / Context files / etc · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/0xdevalias/f40bc5a6f84c4c5ad862e314894b2fa6"},{"title":"AI agent best practices - Help Center","url":"https://help.make.com/ai-agent-best-practices"},{"title":"AI Agent Rule Files Chaos - AI Development Blog | EveryDev.ai","url":"https://www.everydev.ai/p/blog-ai-coding-agent-rules-files-fragmentation-formats-and-the-push-to-standardize"},{"title":"Use custom instructions in VS Code","url":"https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/customization/custom-instructions"},{"title":"LLM Agents | Prompt Engineering Guide","url":"https://www.promptingguide.ai/research/llm-agents"},{"title":"AI rules for Flutter and Dart","url":"https://docs.flutter.dev/ai/ai-rules"},{"title":"Using YAML Files To Define Tasks And Parameters For AI Agents » Empathy First Media","url":"https://empathyfirstmedia.com/yaml-files-ai-agents/"},{"title":"Model Configuration :: LocalAI","url":"https://localai.io/advanced/model-configuration/"},{"title":"Rules for AI · zed-industries/zed · Discussion #26550","url":"https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/discussions/26550"},{"title":"LLM agents - Agent Development Kit","url":"https://google.github.io/adk-docs/agents/llm-agents/"}]

Based on the search results, here's an overview of AI agent configuration file naming conventions for LLM rules and prompts:

## Common Configuration File Naming Conventions

### Tool-Specific Files

**CLAUDE.md**: Used by Claude (Anthropic) as a signal to scan the repo and use this file for context.(no content)It's suggested in their documentation and blog posts.(no content)**Cursor**: Uses individual `.mdc` files in a specific folder (i.e., `.cursor/rules/`) which dictate how the AI should behave.(no content)File-naming conventions often include numeric prefixes (001-style.mdc, 200-testing.mdc) to organize rule sets.(no content)**VS Code Copilot**: Workspace instructions files are stored in the `.github/instructions` folder and use the `.instructions.md` extension.(no content)**AGENTS.md**: If you work with multiple AI agents in your workspace, you can define custom instructions for all agents in an AGENTS.md Markdown file at the root(s) of the workspace. VS Code applies the instructions in this file automatically to all chat requests.(no content)**Aider**: Uses `.aider.conf.json` (or `aider.conf.json`) which can include config such as files to include/exclude, model settings, etc.(no content)**Replit**: Supports a `.replit.md` file for project-specific agent instructions.(no content)**Claude Code**: Uses `~/.claude.json` for global config (including MCP servers).

### Emerging Standards

**AGENT.md**: Sourcegraph's AGENT.md spec proposes a universal, hierarchical Markdown-based file for project guidance.(no content)**llms.txt**: A proposal inspired by robots.txt, where websites host an llms.txt file listing key Markdown docs for AI agents to read and index.(no content)There's a growing informal convention for files that help tune LLM behavior, including: **AI.md** or **AI_INSTRUCTIONS.md** (general-purpose file to guide any AI tooling), **CONTRIBUTING.md** (many LLMs are trained to respect these as guidance), and **README.ai.md** (separate AI-focused readme).

### YAML-Based Configurations

**prompt.config.json / agent.config.json**: Custom LLM wrappers, especially those built with LangChain, Autogen, or AgentScript, sometimes define `.config` or `.prompt.*` files in root for behavior tuning.(no content)**Continue**: Uses `config.yaml` specification. Assistants can be loaded from the Hub or locally in your global `.continue` folder (`~/.continue` on Mac).

### Migration/Compatibility

To support multiple tools: "From Claude Code: `mv CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md && ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md`" and "From Cursor: `mv .cursorrules AGENTS.md && ln -s AGENTS.md .cursorrules`".(no content)The ecosystem is just beginning to coalesce around shared formats like AGENT.md, .chatmode.md, .mdc, and llms.txt.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
Agora tenho informações suficientes para fornecer uma análise completa. Deixe-me consolidar os padrões encontrados.

---

# Análise de Convenções de Nomenclatura de Arquivos Unix/Linux para Configurações

## 1. Padrões de Dotfiles

### `.file` (Arquivo Oculto)
- **Origem**: Convenção Unix desde os anos 1970
- **Comportamento**: Oculto do `ls` padrão (requer `ls -a`)
- **Exemplos**: `.gitignore`, `.env`, `.editorconfig`
- **Reconhecimento**: Universal em sistemas Unix-like

### `.filerc` (Run Commands)
- **Origem**: "Run Commands" do MIT CTSS, adotado pelo Unix
- **Significado original**: "run commands" (não "resource configuration" - isso é reinterpretação retroativa)
- **Uso**: Configuração de shell e inicialização de aplicações
- **Exemplos**: `.bashrc`, `.vimrc`, `.zshrc`, `.npmrc`
- **Reconhecimento**: Extremamente alto, especialmente para ferramentas de linha de comando

### `.file.d/` (Diretório Modular)
- **Propósito**: Evitar arquivos monolíticos dividindo configurações
- **Padrão**: Múltiplos arquivos processados juntos
- **Exemplos**: `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/`, `/etc/conf.d/`
- **Reconhecimento**: Padrão em configurações de sistema Linux

### `.file.conf` / `.file.cfg`
- **Uso**: Configurações mais formais, geralmente do sistema
- **Exemplos**: `nginx.conf`, `ssh_config`
- **Reconhecimento**: Alto em contexto de administração de sistemas

---

## 2. Padrões de Diretório Especiais

### `/etc/X.d/` (Configurações Modulares do Sistema)
- **Propósito**: Permite que pacotes instalem configurações sem editar um único arquivo
- **Vantagem**: Fácil gerenciamento e remoção de configurações por pacote

### `~/.config/X/` (Padrão XDG)
- **Especificação**: [XDG Base Directory](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir/latest/)
- **Estrutura**:
  - `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (default: `~/.config/`) - Configurações do usuário
  - `$XDG_DATA_HOME` (default: `~/.local/share/`) - Dados da aplicação
  - `$XDG_CACHE_HOME` (default: `~/.cache/`) - Cache
  - `$XDG_STATE_HOME` (default: `~/.local/state/`) - Estado persistente
- **Formato típico**: `~/.config/<application>/config.yaml`
- **Reconhecimento**: Padrão moderno recomendado, mas adoção inconsistente

### `.local/` Hierarchy
- **Uso**: Instalações e dados locais do usuário
- **Estrutura**: Espelha `/usr/` (`bin/`, `lib/`, `share/`)

---

## 3. Padrões de Nomenclatura Semântica

### UPPERCASE (Arquivos Meta do Projeto)

| Arquivo | Propósito | Reconhecimento |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| `README` | Documentação principal | Universal |
| `LICENSE` | Termos legais | Universal |
| `MANIFEST` | Lista de arquivos do pacote | Alto |
| `Makefile` | Instruções de build | Universal |
| `Dockerfile` | Build de container | Muito alto |
| `Procfile` | Declaração de processos (Heroku) | Alto em PaaS |
| `Vagrantfile` | Configuração de VM | Médio |
| `CODEOWNERS` | Responsáveis por código | Alto (GitHub/GitLab) |
| `CONTRIBUTING` | Guia de contribuição | Alto |
| `CHANGELOG` | Histórico de mudanças | Alto |

**Característica**: Capitalização total ou PascalCase sinaliza "arquivo especial/meta"

### Padrão `Xfile` (Heroku/Ruby Style)
- **Formato**: `{Propósito}file`
- **Exemplos**: `Gemfile`, `Rakefile`, `Procfile`, `Brewfile`
- **Reconhecimento**: Muito alto no ecossistema Ruby/DevOps

---

## 4. Padrão Python Dunder (`__name__`)

### Uso em Arquivos
- `__init__.py` - Marca diretório como pacote Python
- `__main__.py` - Permite execução como script
- `__pycache__/` - Cache de bytecode

### Características
- **Significado**: "Dunder" = "Double UNDERscore"
- **Reservado**: [PEP 8](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/) adverte: "Never invent such names; only use them as documented"
- **Reconhecimento**: Altíssimo em Python, baixo fora dele

### Problema para Uso Genérico
- Fortemente associado a Python
- Pode causar confusão com mecanismos internos de Python
- Não é semanticamente neutro

---

## 5. Padrões Emergentes para Agentes AI

Segundo [análise recente do ecossistema](https://www.everydev.ai/p/blog-ai-coding-agent-rules-files-fragmentation-formats-and-the-push-to-standardize):

| Ferramenta | Arquivo | Formato |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Claude Code | `CLAUDE.md` | Markdown |
| Cursor | `.cursor/rules/*.mdc` | MDC |
| VS Code Copilot | `.github/instructions/*.instructions.md` | Markdown |
| Aider | `.aider.conf.json` | JSON |
| Replit | `.replit.md` | Markdown |
| Sourcegraph | `AGENT.md` (proposta) | Markdown |
| Genérico | `AI.md`, `AGENTS.md` | Markdown |

### Tendências Observadas
1. **Markdown como formato dominante** para regras legíveis por humanos
2. **AGENTS.md** emergindo como convenção multi-ferramenta
3. **llms.txt** proposto como análogo a robots.txt

---

## 6. Análise: Qual Padrão para "Agent Rules"?

### Candidatos Avaliados

| Candidato | Prós | Contras | Score |
|-----------|------|---------|-------|
| `.agentrc` | Segue convenção Unix `.rc`; familiar | Sugere "run commands" não "rules"; menos moderno | 6/10 |
| `.agent/rules` | Modular; XDG-like; extensível | Menos descobrível; requer diretório | 7/10 |
| `AGENTRULES` | Visível; semântico; segue `CODEOWNERS` | Sem extensão; pode confundir | 5/10 |
| `agent.rules` | Claro; extensão semântica | Não segue convenção estabelecida | 4/10 |
| `.rules` | Simples; oculto | Muito genérico; não indica "agent" | 3/10 |
| `__rules__` | Distintivo | Fortemente Python; conflito com dunders | 2/10 |
| `AGENTS.md` | Emergente padrão; compatível multi-tool | Não é "rules" específico | 8/10 |
| `.claude/rules/` | Específico; modular; oficial | Lock-in de ferramenta | 7/10 |

### Recomendação

**Para máximo reconhecimento e compatibilidade futura:**

1. **Se específico para Claude**: Use `.claude/rules/` ou `CLAUDE.md` (já é o padrão oficial)

2. **Se agnóstico de ferramenta**: Use `AGENTS.md` - está emergindo como padrão universal com suporte de múltiplas ferramentas

3. **Se quiser inovação semântica**: Use `.agent/rules.md` - combina:
   - Padrão XDG modular (`.agent/`)
   - Extensão semântica (`.md` para Markdown)
   - Nomeação clara (`rules`)

4. **Evitar `__rules__`**: Padrão Python reservado, causará confusão e viola [PEP 8](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/)

---

## Fontes

- [What Does 'rc' Mean in Dot Files? - LinuxVox](https://linuxvox.com/blog/what-does-rc-mean-in-dot-files/)
- [XDG Base Directory Specification](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir/latest/)
- [XDG Base Directory - ArchWiki](https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/XDG_Base_Directory)
- [Single and Double Underscores in Python Names – Real Python](https://realpython.com/python-double-underscore/)
- [PEP 8 – Style Guide for Python Code](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/)
- [About code owners - GitHub Docs](https://docs.github.com/articles/about-code-owners)
- [Prettier Configuration](https://prettier.io/docs/configuration)
- [AI Agent Rule Files Chaos - EveryDev.ai](https://www.everydev.ai/p/blog-ai-coding-agent-rules-files-fragmentation-formats-and-the-push-to-standardize)
- [Notes on AI Agent Rule Files - GitHub Gist](https://gist.github.com/0xdevalias/f40bc5a6f84c4c5ad862e314894b2fa6)
- [Custom Instructions in VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/customization/custom-instructions)
